questions.specification: What is the main effect of eutrophication in aquatic ecosystems?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.8 Eutrophication of aquatic and marine ecosystems due to leaching
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.8 Eutrophication of aquatic and marine ecosystems due to leaching
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the main effect of eutrophication in aquatic ecosystems, specifically oxygen depletion due to increased decomposition—covered under eutrophication of aquatic and marine ecosystems due to leaching.

options.content: Increase in water temperature
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Eutrophication primarily affects dissolved oxygen via algal blooms and decomposition, not temperature.

options.content: Decrease in fish predation
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Eutrophication does not directly cause decreased fish predation; it can reduce fish populations due to hypoxia.

options.content: Drop in oxygen due to increased decomposition
options.markscheme: Correct. Nutrient enrichment can cause algal blooms; decomposition of dead biomass by bacteria increases oxygen demand, lowering dissolved oxygen.

options.content: Improved biodiversity and oxygen production
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Eutrophication typically reduces biodiversity and can cause oxygen depletion rather than improving oxygen levels.

options.content: They decrease the total amount of short-wave solar radiation that is reflected by the upper atmosphere.

questions.specification:
Ecologists monitored the development of an ecosystem on a newly formed volcanic island over a period of 150 years. The study tracked changes in biomass and energy flow as the community underwent primary succession.

| Stage | Years since formation | Total Biomass ($kg\ m^{-2}$) | Gross Primary Productivity ($g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$) | Respiration ($g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pioneer | 10 | 0.2 | 150 | 90 |
| Intermediate | 50 | 4.5 | 800 | 450 |
| Climax | 150 | 18.2 | 1200 | 1150 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.13 Changes occurring during primary succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.13 Changes occurring during primary succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is explicitly about primary succession on a newly formed volcanic island, tracking biomass changes across pioneer/intermediate/climax stages and asking about how conditions change during succession.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C4.2 Transfer of energy and matter > C4.2.15 Primary production as accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by autotrophs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.2.15 Primary production as accumulation of carbon compounds in biomass by autotrophs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Calculating NPP from GPP and respiration and discussing changing energy storage as climax is approached directly tests primary production/biomass accumulation concepts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.15 Climax communities and arrested succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.15 Climax communities and arrested succession (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: References to the ecosystem approaching a climax community and comparison of energy flow/respiration in the climax stage align with climax community characteristics.

parts.content: State the relationship between time and total biomass during this process of primary succession.
parts.markscheme:
- Total biomass increases with time during succession (positive correlation). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomass is lowest in the pioneer stage and highest in the climax stage (increasing trend across stages). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the net primary productivity (NPP) for the intermediate stage. Then discuss how energy storage (biomass accumulation/NPP) changes from the intermediate stage to the climax community.
parts.markscheme:
- NPP calculation: $\text{NPP} = \text{GPP} - R = 800 - 450 = 350\ g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct units stated for NPP: $g\ m^{-2}\ yr^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- From intermediate to climax, respiration increases substantially (higher community maintenance costs). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the climax stage, $\text{GPP} \approx R$ (e.g. $1200$ vs $1150$), so NPP is low and little additional energy is stored as new biomass. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Biomass is still high in the climax community because it has accumulated over time, even though net biomass gain per year is small. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how pioneer species, such as lichens and mosses, alter the abiotic environment to allow for the establishment of later successional stages.
parts.markscheme:
- Physical and/or chemical weathering: pioneer organisms break down bare rock to produce smaller particles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Organic matter/humus addition: death and decomposition of pioneers adds organic material, improving water retention and soil structure. <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Nutrient enrichment: some pioneers (e.g. cyanobacteria in lichens) fix nitrogen, increasing soil fertility. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award any two of the above points. <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the percentage change in deforestation from July 2022 to July 2023. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the values into the percentage change formula: $\frac{500 - 1500}{1500} \times 100$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the percentage change: $-66.7\%$ (accept values between $-66.6\%$ and $-67.0\%$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Use Figure b to calculate the approximate area of the Amazon rainforest in Peru. Show your working.

Note: Total Amazon area $= 7.76 \text{ million } \text{km}^2$.
parts.markscheme: - Identify Peru's percentage ($10\%$) and calculate the area: $0.10 \times 7.76 \times 10^6 = 776{,}000\ \text{km}^2$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two ecosystem features that support long-term rainforest stability.
parts.markscheme:
- High biodiversity (species interdependence) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Nutrient cycling (e.g., rapid decomposition) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Canopy layering (niche diversity) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using Figure a and Figure b, predict how deforestation may reduce the long-term stability of the Amazon rainforest ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Biodiversity loss: habitat fragmentation leading to extinction cascades and reduced ecosystem resilience <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Carbon cycle disruption: reduced $\text{CO}_2$ sequestration contributes to global climate change and further stress on the ecosystem <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hydrological impact: reduced transpiration leads to altered rainfall patterns and an increased risk of droughts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Soil degradation/nutrient loss (e.g., erosion and reduced nutrient recycling) reducing primary productivity and slowing recovery <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A long-term ecological study monitored the species diversity of a forest ecosystem before and after a wildfire. Simpson's Diversity Index ($D$) was used to measure biodiversity at different time points. The results are shown below:

| Year | Simpson’s Diversity Index ($D$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $2000$ (before fire) | $0.85$ |
| $2001$ (immediately after fire) | $0.40$ |
| $2005$ | $0.55$ |
| $2010$ | $0.70$ |
| $2020$ | $0.82$ |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.12 Ecological succession and its causes (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.12 Ecological succession and its causes (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) explicitly requires the use of ecological succession to explain the ecosystem's recovery after a wildfire.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.1 Stability as a property of natural ecosystems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.1 Stability as a property of natural ecosystems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for an explanation of the trend in relation to the stability of the ecosystem, which is a core property of natural ecosystems.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A4.2 Conservation of biodiversity > A4.2.1 Biodiversity as the variety of life in all its forms, levels and combinations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A4.2.1 Biodiversity as the variety of life in all its forms, levels and combinations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question utilizes Simpson's Diversity Index as a quantitative measure of biodiversity to monitor the ecosystem over time.

parts.content: Describe the trend in species diversity over time and explain what it suggests about the stability of the ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe the trend as a sharp decrease in $D$ (from $0.85$ to $0.40$) in $2001$ followed by a gradual recovery reaching $0.82$ by $2020$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that the recovery of $D$ to near pre-fire levels indicates the ecosystem is resilient and possesses long-term stability. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the concept of ecological succession, explain how the ecosystem recovered after the wildfire.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that secondary succession occurs after the fire because soil and a seed bank remain. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that the progression from pioneer species (e.g., grasses) to more complex communities (e.g., shrubs and trees) increases species diversity as more niches are created. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
The distribution of salt marsh plants is often determined by their tolerance to environmental stressors. A study was conducted along a belt transect to observe the density of the annual sea-blite (*Suaeda maritima*) in relation to soil salinity (ppm = mg kg$^{-1}$ soil) at varying distances from the high-tide mark.

| Distance from high-tide mark (m) | Soil salinity (ppm) | Density of *S. maritima* (individuals per $m^2$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 35,000 | 4 |
| 10 | 28,000 | 12 |
| 20 | 22,000 | 25 |
| 30 | 15,000 | 18 |
| 40 | 8,000 | 2 |

_**OR**_

_**OR**_

parts.markscheme:

_**OR**_

_**OR**_

<note>Award a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>.</note>

parts.content: Distinguish between the fundamental niche and the realized niche of a species like *S. maritima*.
parts.markscheme:
- **Fundamental niche**: the potential mode of existence/full range of environmental conditions under which a species can survive and reproduce <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Realized niche**: the actual mode of existence/narrower range of conditions occupied due to limiting factors such as competition or predation <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.9 Biomagnification of pollutants in natural ecosystems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.9 Biomagnification of pollutants in natural ecosystems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks to outline the process of biomagnification and discuss its impact using DDT, which is the specific subject matter covered under topic D4.2.9.

parts.content: Outline the process of biomagnification and discuss its impact using DDT as an example.
parts.markscheme:
- Biomagnification is the process by which the concentration of a substance, such as a pesticide, increases in the tissues of organisms at each successive level of the food chain. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- DDT is a pesticide that, when introduced into an ecosystem, is absorbed by organisms at the base of the food chain, such as plankton. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- As these organisms are consumed by predators, the concentration of DDT increases in the bodies of these predators. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This process continues up the food chain, leading to the highest concentrations in top predators, such as birds of prey. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The impact of DDT biomagnification includes thinning of eggshells in birds, leading to reduced reproductive success. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- There is potential harm to other wildlife and humans who consume affected organisms. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: The process of eutrophication is a significant environmental issue affecting water quality and aquatic life globally.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.2 Sustainability and change > D4.2.8 Eutrophication of aquatic and marine ecosystems due to leaching
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.2.8 Eutrophication of aquatic and marine ecosystems due to leaching
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The entire question focuses on the process of eutrophication, its definition, its mechanistic effect on oxygen levels via leaching and decomposer activity, and its specific impacts on aquatic and marine ecosystems, which is the direct subject of this leaf topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A - Unity and Diversity > A4.2 Conservation of biodiversity > A4.2.6 Causes of the current biodiversity crisis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A4.2.6 Causes of the current biodiversity crisis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) specifically asks to evaluate the consequences of eutrophication on biodiversity. Eutrophication is a major anthropogenic cause of the current biodiversity crisis and ecosystem degradation mentioned in this topic.

parts.markscheme:
- **Define** eutrophication as the process where water bodies become enriched with nutrients, leading to excessive growth of algae and other aquatic plants <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content:
To define eutrophication using a standard academic approach, we must identify two key components:
1. The **input**: Enrichment of the water with nutrients.
2. The **response**: The resulting excessive growth of plant life.

This process is a major part of **Topic D4.2: Sustainability and change**, which examines how human activities like agriculture impact ecosystem stability.

steps.title: Identify nutrient enrichment
steps.content: The first part of the definition focuses on the cause. Eutrophication occurs when a water body receives an excess of nutrients, typically nitrates and phosphates. These often enter the water through leaching or runoff from fertilizers used in farming.

steps.title: Describe the biological response
steps.content: The second part of the definition describes the effect of those nutrients. Because nitrogen and phosphorus are usually limiting factors for growth, their sudden abundance causes a population explosion of primary producers, such as algae and macro-algae (often called an algal bloom).

highlights.text: leading to excessive growth of algae and other aquatic plants

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
Combining these elements into a concise academic statement provides the full definition required for the mark.

**Eutrophication** is the process where water bodies become enriched with nutrients, leading to excessive growth of algae and other aquatic plants.

highlights.text: Define eutrophication as the process where water bodies become enriched with nutrients, leading to excessive growth of algae and other aquatic plants

parts.content: Explain how the process of eutrophication affects oxygen availability in aquatic ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** that the process begins with nutrient enrichment from sources such as runoff containing fertilizers, sewage, or detergents <marks>M1</marks>
- **Explain** that when these plants and algae die, their decomposition by aerobic bacteria consumes dissolved oxygen, leading to hypoxic conditions <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Start with nutrient enrichment
steps.content:
The process begins with **nutrient enrichment**. Excess nutrients, specifically nitrates and phosphates, enter the water body. This usually happens through runoff containing fertilizers from farmland, or from sewage and detergents being released into the ecosystem.

This is a key concept covered in **Topic D4.2: Sustainability and Change**, specifically regarding how human activities like agriculture impact ecosystem stability.

highlights.text: nutrient enrichment from sources such as runoff containing fertilizers, sewage, or detergents

steps.title: The resulting algal bloom
steps.content: Because these nutrients are often limiting factors in aquatic environments, their sudden abundance triggers a rapid increase in the population of algae and aquatic plants, known as an **algal bloom**. This bloom can cover the surface of the water, preventing light from reaching plants deeper down.

steps.title: Death and microbial decomposition
steps.content: Eventually, the nutrients are depleted or light becomes restricted, causing the algae and plants to die. As they die, they sink to the bottom where they are broken down by **aerobic bacteria** (decomposers).

steps.title: Oxygen consumption and hypoxia
steps.content:
The final link in the chain is the loss of oxygen. These aerobic bacteria require oxygen for **cell respiration** to break down the organic matter. Because there is a massive amount of dead material to decompose, the bacteria multiply and consume dissolved oxygen at a rate much higher than it can be replenished.

$$\text{Organic matter} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Bacteria}} CO_2 + H_2O + \text{Energy}$$

This leads to **hypoxic** (low oxygen) or even anoxic (no oxygen) conditions, making the water unable to support fish and other aquatic life.

highlights.text: decomposition by aerobic bacteria consumes dissolved oxygen, leading to hypoxic conditions

steps.title: Identify the initial trigger
steps.content: Eutrophication begins when a water body receives an excess of nutrients, typically nitrates and phosphates. This usually occurs through leaching or runoff from agricultural land (fertilizers) or sewage discharge. This initial stage is known as nutrient enrichment.

highlights.text: nutrient enrichment from sources such as runoff containing fertilizers, sewage, or detergents

steps.title: Algal bloom and death
steps.content: These extra nutrients cause a rapid increase in the population of algae, known as an **algal bloom**. While the algae produce oxygen via photosynthesis during the day, they eventually die off once the nutrients are depleted or light is blocked by the dense surface layer. This creates a massive influx of dead organic matter.

steps.title: Increased Biological Oxygen Demand
steps.content:
The sudden abundance of dead organic matter provides a food source for **aerobic decomposers** (bacteria). As these bacteria break down the organic matter, their population increases rapidly, leading to a surge in **aerobic cell respiration**. 

This process significantly increases the **Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD)**—the amount of dissolved oxygen needed by aerobic biological organisms to break down organic material in the water.

steps.title: Resulting hypoxic conditions
steps.content: Because the decomposers consume dissolved oxygen at a rate much faster than it can be replenished from the atmosphere or photosynthesis, the oxygen levels in the water plummet. This leads to **hypoxia** (low oxygen) or even **anoxia** (no oxygen), which causes fish and other aquatic organisms to suffocate.

highlights.text: decomposition by aerobic bacteria consumes dissolved oxygen, leading to hypoxic conditions

parts.content: Evaluate the consequences of eutrophication on biodiversity in aquatic and marine ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that excessive plant growth/algal blooms can block sunlight, leading to the death of submerged aquatic plants and affecting the organisms that depend on them <marks>A1</marks>
- **Describe** how the resulting decrease in oxygen levels (hypoxia) causes the death of fish and other aquatic organisms, thereby reducing species richness <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** that the ecosystem imbalance can lead to the dominance of a few tolerant species over others, further reducing the overall biodiversity of the system <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Initial growth and light blockage
steps.content:
The sequential chain starts when excess nutrients (like nitrates and phosphates) enter the water, causing an **algal bloom**. This dense layer of algae at the surface blocks sunlight from reaching the deeper parts of the water. 

Without sunlight, submerged aquatic plants cannot perform photosynthesis and eventually die. This directly reduces biodiversity by eliminating these primary producers and the specific habitats or food sources they provide for other organisms.

highlights.text: algal blooms can block sunlight, leading to the death of submerged aquatic plants

steps.title: Decomposition and oxygen depletion
steps.content:
As the algae and submerged plants die, they become a massive food source for aerobic bacteria (decomposers). These bacteria multiply rapidly and consume vast amounts of dissolved oxygen ($O_2$) through cell respiration. 

This leads to a state of **hypoxia** (critically low oxygen levels). Because most aquatic animals, such as fish and macroinvertebrates, require oxygen to survive, this depletion leads to mass mortality events. This significantly reduces **species richness** in the ecosystem.

highlights.text: decrease in oxygen levels (hypoxia) causes the death of fish and other aquatic organisms

steps.title: Loss of ecosystem balance
steps.content:
The final stage in the causal chain is a shift in the community structure. While sensitive species die off, a few **tolerant species** (such as certain types of worms or larvae that can handle low oxygen) may survive and dominate. 

This creates a severe ecosystem imbalance. Instead of a diverse, complex web of many species, the system becomes dominated by just a few, further reducing the overall biodiversity and the resilience of the aquatic environment.

highlights.text: dominance of a few tolerant species over others, further reducing the overall biodiversity

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must trace the impact through these levels:
1. **A1**: Mention light blockage and the death of submerged plants.
2. **A1**: Link oxygen depletion (hypoxia) to the death of fish and reduction in species richness.
3. **A1**: Explain the shift toward a few tolerant species, resulting in an overall loss of biodiversity.

steps.title: Analyze the Trophic Impact
steps.content:
To evaluate the consequences of eutrophication, we use **Trophic Level Analysis**. This means we look at how the oversupply of nutrients (like nitrates and phosphates) disrupts each level of the food web, starting from the primary producers and moving up to the top consumers. 

This process is covered in **Topic D4.2: Sustainability and Change** of your IB Biology syllabus.

highlights.text: Eutrophication of aquatic and marine ecosystems due to leaching

steps.title: Loss of Primary Producers
steps.content:
The first trophic consequence occurs at the surface. Rapid nutrient enrichment causes **algal blooms**. These dense layers of algae block sunlight from penetrating deeper into the water. 

As a result, **submerged aquatic plants** at the bottom cannot perform photosynthesis and die. This represents a significant loss of primary productivity and habitat for other organisms.

highlights.text: algal blooms can block sunlight, leading to the death of submerged aquatic plants

steps.title: Death of Higher-Order Consumers
steps.content:
When the massive amounts of algae and submerged plants die, saprotrophic bacteria decompose the organic matter. This aerobic decomposition consumes vast amounts of dissolved oxygen ($O_2$).

This leads to **hypoxia** (low oxygen levels). Higher-order consumers, such as **fish** and other sensitive aquatic animals, cannot survive in these conditions and die, which drastically reduces the **species richness** of the ecosystem.

highlights.text: decrease in oxygen levels (hypoxia) causes the death of fish and other aquatic organisms, thereby reducing species richness

steps.title: Dominance of Tolerant Specialists
steps.content:
With the loss of both primary producers and sensitive consumers, the ecological niches become vacant. This vacuum is filled by a few **tolerant species** (specialists that can thrive in low-oxygen or high-nutrient environments).

Because only a few species can survive these harsh conditions, they dominate the system. This lack of variety leads to a severe reduction in **overall biodiversity**.

highlights.text: dominance of a few tolerant species over others, further reducing the overall biodiversity
